claim: No major AI coding tool (Cursor, Copilot, Windsurf, Aider, Codex, Continue.dev, Cline) publishes evidence that its learning/memory system measurably reduces error recurrence across sessions.
evidence: Eight tools surveyed in D1a; none have built-in violation trend tracking or published effectiveness data. Industry norm is activity metrics, not outcome metrics.

claim: Claude Code and Windsurf are the only AI coding tools with automatic AI-generated memory (not just human-written rules). Both store memories outside git and have no built-in effectiveness measurement.
evidence: Official documentation for both tools confirmed. Auto Memory introduced in Claude Code v2.1.59. Windsurf documents local-only storage limitation explicitly.

claim: Cursor had automatic memory generation (v0.51) but removed it. Current system requires human effort to codify learnings into rule files.
evidence: Cursor forum posts and changelog confirm introduction and removal. Users told to export memories to rules.

claim: Code-level enforcement and behavioral compliance are structurally asymmetric: code rules can be enforced deterministically; behavioral rules can only be enforced probabilistically.
evidence: GitHub documentation explicitly frames this split. Academic literature (Castellanos 2022) treats process compliance as a distinct subfield. AI compliance testing showed Copilot generated completely wrong output when given complex behavioral instructions.

claim: Claude Code lifecycle hooks can deterministically enforce tool-call patterns (file protection, command blocking, audit trails) but cannot enforce cognitive/conversational patterns (ask before implementing, use convergence loops).
evidence: Official Claude Code hooks documentation states hooks fire on tool-use events, not reasoning/planning decisions. PreToolUse hooks intercept any tool call but cannot intercept the cognitive decision to proceed.

claim: The behavioral rules that resist hook enforcement share one characteristic: they are about cognitive/conversational patterns rather than tool-use patterns.
evidence: Synthesized from Claude Code hooks documentation and the list of CLAUDE.md behavioral guardrails. 8/14 guardrails describe conversation or reasoning patterns, not tool calls.

claim: Behavioral compliance measurement in developer tooling remains unsolved as of 2026. No tool directly measures whether an AI agent followed a behavioral rule (e.g., 'did Claude ask before implementing?').
evidence: Multiple search angles found no tooling for behavioral compliance measurement. DORA, LinearB, Pluralsight Flow measure process outcomes, not behavioral adherence.

claim: A minimum viable learning system that proves its own value requires exactly three components: a baseline, a closed feedback loop, and one time-series trend metric tracked over 60+ days.
evidence: Synthesized across practitioner literature, DX Core 4, Tim Cochran developer effectiveness research. Academic support from ACM TOCE study on automated feedback tools.

claim: The minimum viable metric set for proving pattern enforcement effectiveness is four metrics: new violations per PR (rolling 30-day), recurrence rate per rule, hook adoption rate, and time-to-fix per category.
evidence: D3 findings synthesized from practitioner literature. All four metrics are decrease-over-time signals, all derivable from existing CI/hook infrastructure, all resist Goodhart's Law gaming without genuinely fixing behavior.

claim: Simple measurement systems that run on every commit and produce one number persist; complex dashboards requiring manual data collection are abandoned within 3-6 months.
evidence: Spotify Soundcheck (5 min vs 60 day onboarding), Etsy simple instruments, pre-commit hook case study (50% merge conflict reduction), healthcare SaaS team abandonment after 3 months.

claim: Notion's eslint-seatbelt ratcheting pattern (baseline TSV + CI freeze + automatic count reduction on fix) is the most applicable published pattern for graduating stalled enforcement patterns.
evidence: Open-sourced at github.com/justjake/eslint-seatbelt. Used at scale for multi-year React migration. Directly solves the 'too many existing violations' dilemma without a big-bang migration sprint.

claim: Retroactive measurement of learning system effectiveness IS feasible from existing SoNash data without new instrumentation.
evidence: SZZ algorithm, git pickaxe, PR JSONL analysis, commit message NLP classification all require only existing git history and JSONL files. hook-runs.jsonl is a purpose-built retroactive signal dataset.

claim: hook-runs.jsonl is the highest-value retroactive data source for measuring SoNash learning system effectiveness. A simple groupby over violation_type x session_window produces the required trend lines.
evidence: D4 serendipity finding. File contains structured, timestamped, typed violation data across sessions. Academic MSR researchers would design this from scratch; it already exists.

claim: SoNash's sanitizeError pattern commits did NOT decline after the pattern checker was introduced in January 2026. Monthly counts: Jan=43, Feb=31, Mar=40 — no declining trend.
evidence: Direct git log analysis from D7a. Commits matching sanitize/sanitizeError by month. Rate did not decrease after checker introduction on 2026-01-02.

claim: Pattern/compliance fix commits remained stable at ~30% of all commits from January through March 2026 despite 4+ months of pattern enforcement tooling.
evidence: Direct git log analysis from D7a. Ratio: Jan 30.6%, Feb 30.4%, Mar 30.2%. The ratio did not decline after the pattern checker was introduced.

claim: The learning-router graduation pipeline has a field-name mismatch bug that renders the escalation mechanism completely inert. All 37 entries in pending-refinements.jsonl have generated_code: null.
evidence: Direct code analysis from D7c. buildPendingEntry() looks for entry.scaffold?.generatedCode but learning-router.js never populates a generatedCode field. run-alerts.js skips entries without actionable generated_code.

claim: The confidence classifier hard-codes behavioral routes as 'always low confidence,' meaning 19 of 39 refined entries can never advance to enforced without manual intervention that no mechanism currently delivers.
evidence: Direct code analysis from D7c. confidence-classifier.js rules: behavioral → always low. No tooling delivers behavioral entries to human for judgment.

claim: The sole 'enforced' entry (3689cfd62f77) has a broken test that fails because verify-enforcement.js executes it as a plain Node.js script, not through Jest. The graduation pipeline has never successfully completed end-to-end.
evidence: Direct code analysis from D7c. Test uses Jest describe/test syntax. verify-enforcement.js runs via execFileSync(process.execPath, [realAbsPath]) without Jest context.

claim: analyze-learning-effectiveness.js and learning-routes.jsonl are parallel, disconnected systems. They track 'learning effectiveness' using entirely different data sources and taxonomies with no shared signal.
evidence: Direct code analysis from D7c. analyze-learning-effectiveness.js never reads, writes, or references learning-routes.jsonl. The '89.2% effectiveness' comes from one system; router status from the other.

claim: The '89.2% learning effectiveness' metric is a textbook vanity metric by Lean Startup criteria: not actionable, not auditable, and cannot distinguish enforcement-driven absence from workload-driven absence of patterns.
evidence: Applied Lean Startup 3 A's test (actionable/accessible/auditable). The numerator 'patterns absent in later sessions' cannot be audited to determine causation. Confirmed via Goodhart's Law, McNamara Fallacy, and SPACE framework analysis.

claim: Goodhart's Law applies directly: the 89.2% metric measures correlation (patterns are absent) and labels it causation (learning occurred). A pattern's absence may reflect workload shift, domain change, or genuine enforcement — the metric cannot distinguish these.
evidence: Applied Goodhart's Law documentation from Axify, Jellyfish, Wikipedia. The mechanism is precise: the numerator captures pattern absence, not prevented violations.

claim: The McNamara Fallacy describes the current learning system design: it measures what is easy to count (patterns exist, patterns absent) and treats what is hard to measure (behavioral change, counterfactual) as non-existent.
evidence: InfoWorld direct application of McNamara Fallacy to software development measurement. Steps 1-4 map directly to the learning system design.

claim: Interrupted Time Series (ITS) analysis is the most applicable method for measuring solo developer tool impact without A/B tests. Requires ~12 pre- and 12 post-intervention data points minimum.
evidence: PMC peer-reviewed tutorial on ITS regression. Sagepub single-group ITS study. Multiple PMC sample size studies. Method designed for single-group before/after measurement.

claim: Single-Subject Experimental Designs (SSEDs), specifically the Multiple Baseline variant, provide the strongest within-individual causal evidence when reversal is impractical. Requires 3+ demonstrations of effect.
evidence: PMC peer-reviewed paper on SCED for evidence-based practice. What Works Clearinghouse standards. Multiple Baseline design used when reversal is impractical (cannot un-learn a habit).

claim: The METR 2025 RCT found experienced developers using AI tools took 19% longer on real tasks, while believing AI had sped them up by 20%. The perception gap is directionally inverted.
evidence: METR preprint (16 experienced developers, 246 issues, randomized design). Most rigorous controlled study of AI tool effectiveness for experienced developers.

claim: Nonaka's SECI Internalization quadrant is structurally blocked for Claude: without weight updates, explicit rules can never become tacit. CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md are prosthetic externalization, not true internalization.
evidence: GRAI framework (VINE Journal 2025) extends SECI for generative AI. HAC-SECI model (Springer 2024) proposes dual-loop structure. Official Claude docs confirm CLAUDE.md is advisory, not enforced.

claim: Official Claude Code documentation sets ~200 lines as the CLAUDE.md adherence threshold: longer files consume more context and reduce adherence. Conflicting rules are resolved arbitrarily by Claude.
evidence: Direct citation from official Claude Code documentation (Tier 1 source). Architectural constraint, not theoretical recommendation.

claim: The knowing-doing gap (Pfeffer & Sutton) applies to Claude: more rules do not guarantee more behavioral compliance. The bottleneck is Claude's reliable application of captured knowledge in context, not the capture itself.
evidence: Pfeffer & Sutton 2000 academic book. Applied to AI systems: CLAUDE.md delivers advisory guidance; enforcement is architectural. The gap between rule existence and rule application is the central effectiveness question.

claim: SoNash's learning system operates at single-loop learning (catch error, add rule) with no systematic mechanism for double-loop learning (questioning whether the rule-adding approach itself is working).
evidence: Argyris 1977 HBR framework applied to SoNash. The espoused theory ('feedback gets documented, rules get added') vs. theory-in-use ('rules accumulate, reading friction increases') gap is the central question.

claim: Behavioral SOPs have a ~6-12 month half-life. CLAUDE.md rules added at session 50 may be actively incorrect by session 200 in a rapidly evolving workflow.
evidence: General organizational KM research cited in D10. Specific decay rates for AI behavioral guidance are unknown. Extrapolated from SOP literature.

claim: The 4-phase graduation pipeline (Observe → Codify → Inform → Enforce) is the standard pattern. Most pipelines stall at Phase 3 (Inform) because no owner declares the transition date to Phase 4.
evidence: Semgrep methodology, ESLint ratcheting (Notion), Harness Policy-as-Code all describe the same 4-phase structure. The neugierig.org analysis documents warning blindness as the canonical failure mode.

claim: Facebook Infer fixed 1,000+ bugs per month pre-production with 80% correct-report rate. Meta code improvement (dead code removal) produced 90% decrease in severity-triggering diffs. These are the clearest published 'tool X = metric Y moved' stories.
evidence: CACM paper on Scaling Static Analyses at Facebook. arXiv 2504.12517v1 (2025) Meta code improvement practices study with specific odds ratios.

claim: SonarQube violations have 'significant but small' effect on fault-proneness in empirical study. Clean classes are NOT meaningfully less fault-prone than dirty ones in many categories (Lenarduzzi 2020).
evidence: Peer-reviewed study of 33 Apache Java projects, 726 commits, 27K faults, 95K+ SonarQube items. Published in Journal of Systems and Software.

claim: The 260+ session, non-developer director context is at least one order of magnitude deeper than any documented practitioner case. Existing effectiveness frameworks have limited direct applicability.
evidence: D9 systematic review found no documented practitioner at 200+ session depth using professional agentic CLI tools. The closest case study documented 50 sessions.

claim: Higher AI literacy correlates with MORE overestimation of competence, not less. The METR RCT finding (19% slowdown, 20% perceived speedup) means subjective session-quality self-assessment is systematically unreliable.
evidence: Computers in Human Behavior 2025 study on AI literacy and competence overestimation. METR 2025 RCT (peer-reviewed, randomized design).

claim: The four-layer enforcement architecture (deterministic code/CI, semi-deterministic hooks, probabilistic CLAUDE.md, human review) is the emerging best practice for AI-assisted development teams.
evidence: Synthesized from D2 findings. Codacy/Claude Code integration documentation, Claude Code hooks official docs, GitHub compliance documentation. Not formally named in any single authoritative source.

claim: Retrospective practices have weak empirical support for direct performance improvement. Only 11% of teams rate retrospectives as 'very important' for work output; discussions dominated by recurring opinions rather than productive improvements.
evidence: arXiv 2502.03570 study of 19 teams. Springer empirical study on retrospective discussion content. Frequently cited improvement percentages (30% defect drop) lack traceable primary sources.

claim: The Zalando AI postmortem analysis finding (25% reduction in subsequent datastore incidents) demonstrates that AI pattern analysis value scales with corpus size — a single postmortem has low value, 1,000 analyzed together surfaces actionable systemic patterns.
evidence: Zalando 2025 engineering blog. Specific intervention: automated IaC change validation triggered by postmortem pattern discovery. Self-reported numbers; not independently verified.

claim: The learning curve confound affects before/after tool measurement: new tool adoption creates overhead that degrades performance before improving it. First 2-4 weeks post-introduction should be excluded as adoption period.
evidence: METR 2025 study catalogued 20 potential confounders including learning curve. Six threats to validity table from D5 findings.

claim: The DORA 2025 report shows AI adoption has shifted from negative relationship with throughput (2024) to positive (2025), but AI continues to show negative relationship with software delivery stability.
evidence: Official DORA 2025 report. Population-level data across 33,000+ survey respondents. Note: correlation data, not causal.
